The air hums electric on summer nights.

**[01:47]** Streets stitched with bike le le lanes and wild flowers. Casco Bay sparkling, like a kept promise beyond the pines. We walk barefoot through damp park grass, where car concrete once ruled, laughing under street lights fueled by the sun breathing deep. No smog. No smog, no sorrow. The lighthouses still stand older than memory. They're beams sweeping across the city that finally listens to the tides. Fairy horns echo across murals and open air markets. **[02:33]** Trade seedlings and second chances. Front porches flicker with guitars and open doors. Housing isn't a prize, it's a promise. **[15:24]** of government, especially right now in this country. The local level is where our voices can most clearly be heard. Ours is a local community willing to step up, adapt, and come together even in the most challenging times. And that's clear from South Portland's history. Here's a powerful story that historian Seth Goldstein shared with me recently at the South Portland Historical Society Museum. In April of 1942, the residents of the Cushing's Point neighborhood, the area we now know as our beloved bug light park, received letters from the federal government with limited advance warning.

**[16:11]** Those letters gave them just nine days to leave their homes so the shipyard could expand to support the war effort. Can you imagine being given just nine days for that? Those families had lived there for generations. Their lives and memories were rooted in that neighborhood and it was unfair. And yet it came at a time when people across the country were making sacrifices, planting victory gardens, rationing gasoline, salvaging metal, and believing deeply in a shared purpose. At a time of instability and understandable fear, the families of Cushing's Point gathered their strength,

**[17:00]** accepted compensation that could never replace what was lost and made their sacrifice with courage. Those ordinary South Portlanders mobilized to help the world confront a grave threat. **[17:47]** our conservation committee and our open space committee, helped our city purchase and begin to preserve nearly 40 acres of land near Hinkley Park for public trails and wildlife conservation and education. **[53:28]** favorite stories about her. Things we would never forget. Ways we would remember her. We shared until we were outta breath. I have buried more students since then. Too many drug overdoses, mental health. Too much to say. Some things must change. All of us are running around with a sign around our neck that asks, am I needed? And the answer is yes, we're all needed. And finding out how to make a difference is as simple as looking up saying yes. When I was teaching at SMCC, I had a young man from Afghanistan, rasa. He told me he graduated from Ola Beach High School. I graduated from there. I told him, he then proceeded to tell me

**[54:15]** that his senior year, his parents got an apartment in South Portland. But he didn't want to leave. He wanted to graduate with his friends. A place he knew after already having lost so much. Ms. Sandy was the school secretary. He said she and her husband, Mr. George, let me live with them. My last year. He told me funny stories about his first time living with a dog and about how she cooked the meat. So rare he couldn't eat it. You need to invite her to hear your sacred story at City Hall. I said, and he did. And she came and I met her. But it was not the first time. It was Sandy McSweeney. We had grown up in the same town, graduated high school together.

**[55:02]** Imagine our lives interconnected. Some things should not change. In 2014, I was on an agricultural trip and we were in Warren in Maine. There was a man giving us a tour of his farm about my age. And suddenly I looked up to see a sign that read Spear Farm. Was your mother's name Edna by any chance? I asked him when the crowd broke up. Yes it was. He answered. How did you know? I was stunned. I couldn't believe what I was about to say because I said my dad answered an ad in the Boston Globe in 1940. He was 16 years old and he came here

**[55:49]** and worked as a farm hand for your mom and dad. It's because of that experience that he moved his family from Boston to Maine after the war in 1951, they came back to this farm for their honeymoon. I have pictures of it. My mother didn't know what hit her. She made us laugh when she would say she was constipated for two weeks. She'd never seen an outhouse in her life. I told him the two of us laughed. The world is small and if you just take a minute to talk to someone you don't know, reach out and offer to help. You'll see how small it really is. Loss is hard. Starting over is hard. Change is hard.

**[56:35]** Just recently I met Father Leopold from Burundi at a wedding I officiated. He is a Catholic priest who started an orphanage in his country. We met, we talked, I consider myself a Catholic in recovery. But my concerns with the Catholic church do not take precedent over hungry children. Children who've lost their parents to HIV or other tragedies. It does not dominate over the needs of these kids. So I found a suitcase in my attic, filled it with children's books, holiday decorations, and a donation. And again, I was conscious of my privilege. Rebecca Oliver was my student more than 10 years ago at the community college. She just texted me on Friday night to say,

**[57:23]** and I quote, you were the jumpstart to realizing I was worthwhile and I could fight back and demand I was equal. I cried. Education is not just about reading, writing and arithmetic. It's much more. There are people in our community who are hungry, alone, lost, unhoused, confused. There are people new to our city. Not sure where to turn, who to trust, how the system works. Suffering in many ways, much of the time. Many of my friends like those here tonight. We must remember there is a power inequity. And we need to ask, is this the change we want?

**[58:11]** I'm a cat lover and I was reading about room eight, the name of the cat who lived in a schoolhouse for more than 16 years. In the writeup it said, and I quote, schools or communities built on routine, kindness and belonging. Because every day, hundreds of people show up to the same place, see the same faces and following the same rhythms. Room eight wanted that he wanted to belong somewhere. So he chose a school and the school chose him back. He taught routine, showing up every day matters. He taught responsibility. Someone had to feed him. Notice when he was hurt, make sure he got home safely.

**[59:00]** He taught acceptance. He had no invitation, no official role, no purpose except to be himself. And the door stayed open. And most of all, he taught that belonging isn't given. It's created by returning, by trusting, by being welcomed, by showing up day after day, year after year, until you become part of the story, end of the quote from that story. And 70 years later, people still remember, roommate, look up. Our neighbors need us. The stranger on the bus needs us. Our school youth need us. My friends who spoke to you tonight, they need us.

**[59:48]** They need us to remember their faces, hear their voices, and listen to their words. Because it matters. They matter. And we need them. We need them to teach us about the needs of all children, about kindness, openness, and welcoming others about differences, about how to live in a community with an open mind and about how to change this world. This is education too. I can tell you that the Somalis make the best Samsa, the Iraqis, the best doma, the randee, the best. Sobe, the Cambodians, the best noodles, the angolans, the best tilapia and the Iranians, the best rice.

**[60:36]** I have tried it all, but my friends have given me more than that. I have learned about true loss and suffering, about forgiveness, about equity, about change. And it has opened my eyes. Please remember that even in small ways, we all have a chance to make a difference, to educate and be educated. We can all change. We need to create good change. The right change. Charan, Aun, obrigado, Tasha. K. Thank you.

**[61:36]** Uh, thank you very much for coming tonight and for staying and for listening, um, and for being attentive and responsive. Um, I appreciate everyone being here and I look forward to the next year. Um, I look forward to looking out at this audience and not seeing empty chairs, um, but seeing the look of difference and the look of acceptance. Um, and so I thank you and I will accept a motion to adjourn. Oh, somebody, uh, actually you need to say something, Daniel. I thought I was seconding. No. Um, I moved for the inaugural address to be, uh, spread on the record. Seconded. Okay. And all those in favor? Okay. And that's unanimous.

**[62:26]** And now a motion to adjourn. Move to adjourn. Move to adjourn by, uh, member Dowling. And seconded by member Feller. All those in favor of adjournment and we are hereby adjourn. Thank you very much. I think we have to give it. Likewise. Congratulations.
